Flagship Innovation Project

Turning Coastal Waste
Into Community Wealth

🌊
TIDESHIFT
Community Enterprise · Movement

TIDESHIFT is a zero-waste circular enterprise model designed for coastal fishing communities in Indonesia. Named from "shifting the tide" — reversing the cycle of pollution and poverty that defines most of Indonesia's 10,000+ coastal villages.

Inspired by Cangkring Community, Kalibaru — North Jakarta. After conducting firsthand field research and hands-on learning with the community, TIDESHIFT aims to replicate and scale this proven model in Pantai Tanjung Kait, Tangerang — and beyond.
Zero-Waste Flow
🐚 Shell Waste
→
⚙️ Grind & Sieve
→
🧱 Coarse: Products
+
🌱 Fine: Feed & Fertilizer
🔄
KELOOP
Product Line · TIDESHIFT

KELOOP (from kerang + loop) is the product family born from TIDESHIFT's zero-waste process. The name embodies circular economy: shells that once polluted beaches now loop back as valuable products — nothing wasted, everything transformed.

Current Products ✓
Shell-Lime Paving Block
Decorative Perforated Brick
Squat Toilet Pan
Bird Feed Supplement
Handcraft Décor
Future Products (Roadmap)

Plant Pot

Background

The Person
Behind TIDESHIFT

Psychology × Sustainability × Community

I'm Firdatul Janiah, a 26-year-old sustainability innovator from Tangerang, Indonesia. My academic path has been intentionally interdisciplinary — starting with Environmental Engineering at Trisakti University before transitioning to Psychology at Pelita Harapan University — because I believe the hardest part of sustainability isn't the technology, it's the people.

My connection to coastal communities is personal. I grew up near Pantai Tanjung Kait, where I watched fishing families' incomes rise and fall with the seasons, and watched shell waste pile up on beaches no one claimed responsibility for. TIDESHIFT isn't a concept I invented in a classroom — it's a response to something I've seen with my own eyes my entire life.

Through years of involvement in G20 youth diplomacy, environmental campaigns, marine conservation volunteering, and community innovation programs, I've developed one core belief: sustainable change only sticks when the community owns it.
